core_concepts: Specificity Principle: The squat builds full-body strength, stability, and athletic coordination (transferable to jumping/sprinting), while the leg press is a pure isolation/hypertrophy tool.

Internal vs. External Stability: Squats require 'internal' stabilization (core, back, balance), whereas leg presses provide 'external' stability via the machine, allowing for higher force output without systemic fatigue.
Limiting Factor Rule: In squats, the core and lower back often reach failure before the legs; in leg presses, the quads are almost always the true limiting factor.

Complementary Programming: Squats and leg presses are not interchangeable but complementary. Match your leg press foot position to offset your squat bias (e.g., if you high-bar squat for quads, use a wide/high-foot stance on the leg press for glutes).

Anatomical Constraints: Limb proportions (femur length) dictate squat mechanics. Long-femured lifters often find squats become 'good mornings,' necessitating heel elevation (Stiletto Squat) or leg press reliance for quad hypertrophy.

actionable_advice.sets_and_reps: Squat: 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps (Strength focus). Leg Press: 3-4 sets of 10-15 reps (Hypertrophy focus).
actionable_advice.frequency: Perform squats as the primary movement 1-2x/week. Leg press is best as a secondary volume builder (1-3x/week).
actionable_advice.intensity: Squats: 1-2 RIR (risk of failure is higher). Leg Press: 0-1 RIR (safer to push to failure due to machine limits).
actionable_advice.progression_methods: Squats: Prioritize load/technique. Leg Press: Prioritize mechanical tension, control, and incremental set volume.
actionable_advice.recovery_advice: Squats carry high CNS fatigue; recover 48-72h. Leg Press is lower CNS fatigue, easier to recover from but still requires adequate quad recovery.
actionable_advice.exercise_selection: Use squats for functional power; use leg press for safe, high-volume quad isolation without spinal loading.

scientific_claims.claim: Squats promote greater explosive power (jump performance) transfer than leg presses.
Hypertrophy adaptations in quadriceps are similar between squats and leg presses if volume is equated.
Squats elicit higher systemic activation of core and stabilizing musculature.
Leg press allows for higher absolute force production per set due to minimized stability requirements.

coaching_insights: The 'Redundancy Rule': If you back squat with a quad-dominant high-bar style, don't follow it with low-foot-placement leg presses. Balance the stimulus.
For clients with long femurs, if squats are failing to hit the quads, elevate their heels or utilize the leg press as the primary mass-builder.
Leg press is an excellent tool for 'hard sets' because the lack of technical failure (unlike the barbell) lets the legs fully fatigue.
common_mistakes: Substituting squats with leg presses hoping for the same functional strength transfer.
Rounding the lower back on the leg press platform, leading to spinal injury.
Locking out the knees excessively on the leg press, shifting tension to the joints rather than muscle.
Using too much weight on the leg press, resulting in a microscopic range of motion.
Ignoring squat depth due to poor ankle/hip mobility.

myths_addressed: Myth: Leg press is 'cheating' compared to squats.
Myth: You must barbell squat to build big legs.
Myth: Squats are universally bad for your knees.
Myth: Leg presses don't build real-world strength.
Myth: Barbell squats are safer than machines.
Myth: If you squat, you don't need leg press.
Myth: Range of motion doesn't matter on leg press.
Myth: Squats build more quad size than leg press.
Myth: You can't get strong without free weights.

grit_application.goal: Implement a 'Squat-First, Press-Secondary' priority logic based on user goals.
grit_application.routine: If a user selects 'Functional Strength' as a goal, the app prioritizes Squats. If 'Hypertrophy/Quad Focus' is selected, the app prioritizes Leg Press + Stiletto Squats.
grit_application.instruction: Add a 'Limb Proportion' assessment in the G.R.I.T. setup phase; if a user has long femurs, the app suggests heel-elevated squat variations by default.
grit_application.tracking: Log 'Squat Depth' and 'Leg Press Range' separately to ensure users aren't sacrificing range of motion for ego-lifting on the press.
